config.title: Oʻahu
config.coverNote: I built this week around the way your family actually needs Oʻahu to work: a quieter base at Kaimana, one meaningful outing at a time, early dinners, room for the two-year-old to nap, and no day that turns into a race around the island. Every restaurant is marked for a peanut-allergy call before I reserve it. The hotel, flights, and reservations are still placeholders, so this is the shape I would price and confirm with you, not a list pretending to be booked.

days.title: Arrive and let the island come to you
days.summary: Private airport pickup, Kaimana check-in, feet in the sand, and an early dinner downstairs.

days.title: Small sea life and sunset music
days.summary: Walk to the aquarium, leave a real rest window, then see Waikīkī at its best from House Without a Key.

days.title: Windward Oʻahu without the windshield marathon
days.summary: Secret Island gives the kids space to play. Byodo-In adds one quiet cultural stop on the return.

days.title: Hawaiian history and Kakaʻako
days.summary: Bishop Museum first, a proper Hawaiian lunch, murals, then a family-friendly dinner with the best published allergy language of the week.

days.title: A deliberately easy Sunday
days.summary: The zoo before the heat, then a long Kaimana beach and nap window. This is the day that keeps the trip feeling good.

days.title: North Shore at family speed
days.summary: Waimea Valley is the anchor. Haleʻiwa and Waimea Bay are supporting stops, not a checklist that eats the whole day.

days.title: Pearl Harbor and a soft final evening
days.summary: Keep Pearl Harbor focused on the USS Arizona program, return for rest, then close with an early dinner near the hotel.

days.title: Beach walk and flight home
days.summary: A calm final morning, private transfer, and enough airport margin to avoid ending a good week in a rush.

itinerary.title: Nonstop LAX to HNL
itinerary.subtitle: Exact airline, flight, and seats TBC
itinerary.personalNote: I would protect a morning nonstop so arrival day still feels like part of the trip. These times are placeholders until the 2027 schedule and four seats are selected.

itinerary.title: Private airport transfer with child restraints
itinerary.subtitle: One harnessed seat plus one booster
itinerary.personalNote: I would pre-book this rather than start the trip negotiating two child restraints at the curb.

details.Required: Harnessed seat for age 2; booster for age 7

itinerary.title: Kaimana Beach Hotel
itinerary.subtitle: Oceanfront View Junior Suite, proposed
itinerary.personalNote: This is the quiet-base choice from the proposal. The lānai and beach position matter more here than being in the middle of Waikīkī.

itinerary.title: Kaimana Beach reset
itinerary.subtitle: Swim only to current lifeguard conditions
itinerary.personalNote: No first-day excursion. Shoes off, water, sunscreen, and enough time for the children to arrive before dinner.

itinerary.title: Early dinner at Hau Tree
itinerary.subtitle: Peanut-allergy manager confirmation required
itinerary.personalNote: The location is ideal after travel because dinner is downstairs. The menu uses macadamia nuts, almonds, pepitas, tahini, and sesame, so I would only keep this after a direct kitchen conversation.

itinerary.title: Waikīkī Aquarium
itinerary.subtitle: Walk from Kaimana
itinerary.personalNote: This is deliberately small and close. It gives both children a real morning without asking anyone to recover from a major excursion on the first full day.

itinerary.title: Kapiʻolani Park play and picnic
itinerary.personalNote: I would bring known-safe snacks and let the children move. This is more useful than adding another ticketed stop.

itinerary.title: Hotel rest and beach window
itinerary.personalNote: This long middle is intentional. Nap, pool, beach, or nothing. A family itinerary works because the empty space is protected.

itinerary.title: House Without a Key
itinerary.subtitle: Dinner table TBC, allergy call required
itinerary.personalNote: I would book an early dinner table rather than gamble on the first-come lawn. Music currently starts around 5:30 and hula around 6:00, which gives the children the full setting without a late night.

details.Entertainment: Hawaiian music and hula, schedule subject to change

itinerary.title: Private Windward Oʻahu vehicle
itinerary.subtitle: Both child restraints confirmed in writing
itinerary.personalNote: The driver is the structure for the day. It keeps car seats, beach gear, timing, and the return nap from becoming separate problems.

itinerary.title: Kualoa Secret Island morning
itinerary.subtitle: All-ages half-day beach activity
itinerary.personalNote: This is the Kualoa choice that works for both ages. The seven-year-old gets kayaks and beach games, the two-year-old gets sand and shade, and no one has to meet an age minimum for a vehicle tour.

details.Bring: Towels, water shoes, sunscreen, charged power bank

itinerary.title: Known-safe packed lunch
itinerary.subtitle: Do not rely on an unverified tour lunch
itinerary.personalNote: For the allergy, I would bring lunch from a source already cleared in Honolulu. The goal is to remove uncertainty from the most remote meal of the trip.

details.Pack: Sealed safe snacks, simple lunch, water

itinerary.title: Byodo-In Temple
itinerary.subtitle: Quiet return-route stop
itinerary.personalNote: One hour is enough. The temple adds a calm change of pace after the beach without turning the drive home into a circle-island tour.

details.Admission: Current published child rate begins at age 2

itinerary.title: Bishop Museum
itinerary.subtitle: Hawaiian Hall plus Science Adventure Center
itinerary.personalNote: I want this before the island drives. Hawaiian Hall gives the adults context, and the Science Adventure Center gives the children something physical to do with it.

itinerary.title: Highway Inn Kakaʻako
itinerary.subtitle: Hawaiian plate lunch, allergy call required
itinerary.personalNote: This is where I would introduce a broad Hawaiian plate after the museum, when the food has context. I still need a manager to clear the peanut and cross-contact questions.

details.Order idea: Shared Hawaiian plate, exact safe dishes TBC

itinerary.title: Short Kakaʻako mural walk
itinerary.address: Cooke Street and Auahi Street, Honolulu, HI 96813
itinerary.personalNote: Forty minutes, not two hours. Let the seven-year-old pick favorite walls, then go back to the hotel before everyone is done with the heat.

details.Weather: Skip in hard rain or midday heat

itinerary.title: Return to Kaimana for nap and beach
itinerary.personalNote: The museum and lunch are enough. I would protect this reset before dinner.

itinerary.title: Merriman’s Honolulu
itinerary.subtitle: Best published family and allergy language of the week
itinerary.personalNote: Merriman’s publishes that many dishes can be modified for allergies, has a keiki menu, and can prepare a high chair or booster. I would still call a manager and attach the peanut allergy to the reservation in writing.

details.Children: Keiki menu, high chair and booster available

itinerary.title: Honolulu Zoo at opening
itinerary.subtitle: Walk or short ride from Kaimana
itinerary.personalNote: The zoo is the only real assignment today. Go early, let the children set the pace, and leave before the heat decides for you.

itinerary.title: Protected beach and nap afternoon
itinerary.personalNote: This is the easiest day on purpose. Use Kaimana Beach if conditions are calm, or let the afternoon disappear into the room.

itinerary.title: Restock known-safe snacks
itinerary.subtitle: Pack for North Shore tomorrow
itinerary.personalNote: I would use sealed products here, not assume the prepared-food bar is safe. Tomorrow is the day to have more food than you think you need.

details.Buy: Sealed safe snacks, breakfast backup, water
details.Avoid assumption: Prepared bar requires its own cross-contact check

itinerary.title: Simple hotel dinner or cleared repeat
itinerary.subtitle: Choose the restaurant that handled the allergy conversation best
itinerary.personalNote: I would rather repeat a restaurant that earned your trust than chase variety the night before an early driver day.

details.Decision: Hau Tree only if cleared, otherwise a verified repeat or room meal

itinerary.title: Private North Shore vehicle
itinerary.subtitle: Early start, both child restraints
itinerary.personalNote: I would leave before the island traffic builds and keep the driver flexible. Waimea Valley is fixed. Everything else can shrink or disappear if the children need it.

details.Route: Waikīkī, Waimea Valley, Waimea Bay, Haleʻiwa, Waikīkī

itinerary.title: Waimea Valley
itinerary.subtitle: Paved cultural and botanical walk
itinerary.personalNote: This gives the North Shore day a real center. The paved path works with a stroller, and the culture and gardens matter even if weather closes the waterfall swim.

details.Stroller: Collapsible stroller may use shuttle if space permits

itinerary.title: Known-safe North Shore picnic
itinerary.personalNote: This is another place where I would remove the allergy gamble. Pack the meal, then use Haleʻiwa for a walk rather than forcing lunch at the first convenient counter.

itinerary.title: Waimea Bay look-and-decide stop
itinerary.personalNote: The beach is optional, not a promise to swim. Lifeguard flags and the actual ocean decide. A twenty-minute look can be the right answer.

itinerary.title: Haleʻiwa walk
itinerary.personalNote: Keep this loose. Shave ice is only an option after same-day confirmation on peanut ingredients, toppings, scoops, and cross-contact. The walk is still worthwhile without it.

itinerary.title: Private transfer to Pearl Harbor
itinerary.subtitle: Clear bag only
itinerary.personalNote: I would arrive early enough for security without turning the morning into a 6:00 a.m. family drill.

details.Bag policy: Clear bags only; storage available for a fee

itinerary.title: USS Arizona Memorial program
itinerary.subtitle: Timed boat reservation required
itinerary.personalNote: I would keep this respectful and focused. The Arizona program is the reason to come. With a two-year-old, there is no prize for adding every ship and museum afterward.

itinerary.title: Return to Kaimana and keep the afternoon open
itinerary.personalNote: Lunch can be a known-safe repeat or packaged backup. I would leave the emotional morning alone rather than pile another museum on top of it.

itinerary.title: Final dinner at Arden Waikīkī
itinerary.subtitle: Only after direct peanut-allergy clearance
itinerary.personalNote: Arden is close enough to feel easy and local enough to feel like a real final dinner. Its menus include nut ingredients, so I would only book after a manager gives a specific service plan.

details.Fallback: Repeat the restaurant that handled the allergy best

itinerary.title: Final Kaimana beach walk
itinerary.personalNote: Keep the last morning small. One walk, breakfast from the safest known option, then pack without turning departure into a sprint.

itinerary.title: Private transfer to HNL
itinerary.subtitle: Departure time adjusts to final flight
itinerary.personalNote: I would preserve a full airport margin. The exact pickup moves when the return flight is booked.

itinerary.title: Nonstop HNL to LAX
itinerary.subtitle: Exact airline, flight, and seats TBC
itinerary.personalNote: These are planning times, not a selected flight. I would prefer a return that avoids a very early hotel departure and still lands before the children are fully spent.

picks.name: Kaimana Beach Hotel
picks.description: A quieter Waikīkī base directly on Kaimana Beach, proposed for the family.
picks.note: I chose this as the model base because the beach, aquarium, park, and zoo sit close enough to make the week easier. The exact room still needs to be booked.

picks.name: Hau Tree
picks.description: Beachfront restaurant at Kaimana with brunch, pau hana, and dinner.
picks.note: The setting solves arrival night. The menu includes several nut and seed ingredients, so I would not reserve it until the kitchen gives a specific peanut-allergy plan.
picks.hours: Brunch and dinner daily, verify current times

picks.name: Waikīkī Aquarium
picks.description: A compact shoreline aquarium beside Kapiʻolani Park.
picks.note: This is a good first full morning because it is close, contained, and easy to leave when the two-year-old is done.

picks.name: Honolulu Zoo
picks.description: A 42-acre zoo at the edge of Waikīkī.
picks.note: Go at opening and leave before the heat. I would treat this as the whole assignment for the morning, not one stop among five.

picks.name: House Without a Key
picks.description: Halekulani's oceanfront setting for Hawaiian music, hula, and an early family dinner.
picks.note: I would reserve dinner at 5:00 rather than gamble on the first-come lawn. Allergy handling needs a direct call.
picks.hours: Dinner nightly, entertainment schedule subject to change

picks.name: Bishop Museum
picks.description: Hawaiʻi's major cultural and natural-history museum.
picks.note: Hawaiian Hall gives the rest of the island context. The Science Adventure Center keeps this from becoming an adults-only museum morning.

picks.name: Highway Inn Kakaʻako
picks.description: A practical introduction to Hawaiian plate food near the Kakaʻako murals.
picks.note: I would order only after a manager clears the peanut allergy and cross-contact questions. The museum-first sequence gives this lunch more meaning.

picks.name: Merriman’s Honolulu
picks.description: A Ward Village restaurant focused on Hawaiʻi regional cooking, with a keiki menu.
picks.note: This is my strongest first restaurant call because the official FAQ discusses allergy modifications, children, high chairs, and boosters. Confirmation is still required.

picks.name: Arden Waikīkī
picks.description: A chef-driven restaurant on Waikīkī's quieter Diamond Head edge.
picks.note: It is an appealing final dinner because it is close to Kaimana. Nut ingredients appear on the menu, so this is conditional on a manager-approved plan.

picks.name: Kualoa Secret Island
picks.description: An all-ages beach program with kayaks, paddleboards, games, shade, and showers.
picks.note: Choose the half-day, not a rigid full day. Bring a charged power bank because there is no electricity on the beach.
picks.hours: Morning and afternoon half-day sessions, verify 2027 inventory

picks.name: Byodo-In Temple
picks.description: A non-practicing Buddhist temple in the Valley of the Temples.
picks.note: One quiet hour fits well after Kualoa. I would skip it if beach timing runs long rather than rush the stop.

picks.name: Waimea Valley
picks.description: A paved botanical and cultural walk to Waimea Falls.
picks.note: This is the North Shore anchor for this family. The path works with a stroller, and the visit still works if waterfall swimming closes.
picks.hours: Verify current admission and weather status

picks.name: Waimea Bay Beach Park
picks.description: The famous North Shore bay across from Waimea Valley.
picks.note: Treat this as a look-and-decide stop. Lifeguards and actual surf conditions decide whether anyone enters the water.

picks.name: Waiola Shave Ice
picks.description: A longstanding Honolulu shave-ice shop with a positive peanut-allergy traveler report.
picks.note: The report is not current enough to trust blindly. I would call the actual shop and reconfirm syrups, toppings, scoops, and cross-contact before adding it.
picks.hours: Verify current hours and allergy handling

picks.name: Down to Earth Kakaʻako
picks.description: Natural grocery store with packaged allergy-friendly products.
picks.note: Use this for sealed snack and breakfast backups. The prepared-food bar is not automatically safe just because the store carries allergy-friendly products.

picks.name: Koko Head Cafe
picks.description: A busy Kaimukī brunch restaurant with island-influenced dishes.
picks.note: This is an optional brunch, not worth a long wait with two children. Allergy handling and current reservation policy need a call.

picks.name: Mud Hen Water
picks.description: A neighborhood restaurant that reframes local food traditions.
picks.note: I like this as an adults-first food alternative, but I would only add it if the allergy conversation is strong and the family wants a Kaimukī night.

picks.name: Fête
picks.description: A James Beard-recognized Chinatown restaurant built around local farms.
picks.note: This is the town-side dinner alternative. For this trip, the peanut-allergy call matters more than the award.

picks.name: USS Arizona Memorial
picks.description: A free timed memorial program reached by boat from the Pearl Harbor Visitor Center.
picks.note: Reserve the timed program and keep the visit focused. Clear bags only. I would not force every paid historic site into the same morning.
picks.hours: Visitor center currently 7:00 am to 5:00 pm

picks.name: Hanauma Bay Nature Preserve
picks.description: A protected marine-life bay with limited reservation and parking capacity.
picks.note: Optional only. Reservations do not solve every parking and toddler-snorkeling problem, so it is not part of the default week.

picks.name: Diamond Head State Monument
picks.description: An exposed crater hike with stairs and broad coastal views.
picks.note: The seven-year-old may enjoy it, but the two-year-old, heat, stairs, and reservation timing make this a parent-split option rather than a family default.

picks.name: KCC Farmers’ Market
picks.description: A large Saturday farmers' market with produce and prepared foods.
picks.note: Vendors let you ask ingredient questions directly, but crowds and shared preparation still matter. Use sealed produce or clearly verified items.

contacts.name: Gabe
contacts.note: Text me first. If a reservation, weather plan, or allergy conversation needs to move, I will handle it.

contacts.name: Emergency services
contacts.note: For any suspected allergic reaction, use prescribed epinephrine immediately and call 911.

guideBlocks.heading: Peanut allergy plan
guideBlocks.body: I will not label a restaurant allergy-safe from a menu or review. Before each reservation, the manager needs the allergy in writing and must confirm ingredients, sauces, fryer or oil, prep surfaces, desserts, and cross-contact procedures. Repeat it at check-in and with the server. Carry prescribed epinephrine in the day bag, plus a backup dose if your clinician recommends one. Kualoa and North Shore days also get a known-safe packed meal and snacks.

guideBlocks.heading: Getting around with two children
guideBlocks.body: Keep Waikīkī days car-free. The aquarium, zoo, park, beach, and Hau Tree are walkable from Kaimana. Use a private vehicle for Windward Oʻahu, North Shore, Pearl Harbor, and airport transfers. Put one harnessed seat for the two-year-old and one age-appropriate booster for the seven-year-old in writing on every transfer order. Do not assume a rideshare will arrive with both.

guideBlocks.heading: Water and surf judgment
guideBlocks.body: June is usually calmer than winter on the North Shore, but the ocean makes the decision. Check lifeguard flags, current advisories, jellyfish timing, and surf reports every morning. Waimea Bay is a look-and-decide stop. Kaimana remains the easiest reset beach because it is outside the hotel.

guideBlocks.heading: Power and plug compatibility
guideBlocks.body: Hawaiʻi uses the same Type A and Type B outlets as the mainland United States, at 120 volts and 60 hertz. Normal U.S. phone, laptop, camera, and medical-device chargers need no adapter or voltage converter. Keep charging cables, a power bank, and any allergy medication in the carry-on. Secret Island has no electricity, so charge phones and the power bank before leaving Waikīkī.

guideBlocks.heading: What I intentionally left out
guideBlocks.body: I did not force Diamond Head, Hanauma Bay, a full luau, or a one-day circle-island tour into the schedule. Each can be added, but they bring exposed heat, reservation pressure, parking friction, or rigid timing that is hard to justify with a two-year-old when Oʻahu already has better-paced choices.

guideBlocks.heading: Mock itinerary status
guideBlocks.body: Nothing in this hub is represented as booked. TBC means I still need to confirm the flight, room, transfer, ticket, restaurant, price, and cancellation policy. The itinerary is specific enough to price and reserve, but honest about what has not happened yet.

essentials.label: Emergency
essentials.note: Use prescribed epinephrine first for a suspected severe allergic reaction, then call 911.

essentials.label: Pediatric emergency care
essentials.value: Kapiʻolani Medical Center for Women & Children
essentials.note: Reconfirm the most appropriate emergency department with the insurer before travel.

essentials.label: 24-hour pharmacy candidate
essentials.note: Hours can change. Confirm 24-hour status before departure and do not rely on a destination refill for emergency medication.

essentials.label: Allergy-friendly grocery backup
essentials.note: Use sealed packaged foods as the cleanest backup. Treat prepared-food bars as a separate cross-contact decision.
